🚀 Adya.ai Launch Lab Inaugurated at KIT – Empowering the Next Generation of AI Innovators

Kalaignarkarunanidhi Institute of Technology (KIT), Coimbatore proudly inaugurated the Adya.ai Launch Lab, marking an important milestone in strengthening the institution’s commitment to cutting-edge Artificial Intelligence innovation and industry collaboration ,the lab has been inaugurated on March 7,2026

The launch was graced by Shayak Mazumder, Founder & CEO of Adya.ai, a pioneering AI platform company focused on building next-generation agentic AI solutions and intelligent automation systems

The Adya.ai Launch Lab at KIT will serve as a vibrant hub for students to explore advanced AI technologies, develop real-world AI applications, and engage in hands-on innovation using modern AI tools and platforms. This initiative aims to nurture future AI leaders by bridging academia with industry-driven AI research and product development.

Through this collaboration, students will gain exposure to:
🔹 Agentic AI and Generative AI technologies
🔹 Building real-world AI products and applications
🔹 Industry mentorship and startup innovation
🔹 Research-driven learning aligned with global AI trends

We extend our sincere gratitude to Shayak Mazumder and the Adya.ai team for partnering with KIT in this transformative initiative that will empower students to innovate, experiment, and lead in the rapidly evolving AI ecosystem.

✨ A new chapter begins at KIT as we continue to build a strong ecosystem for AI innovation, industry collaboration, and future-ready talent development.

author: Dharshika D.S
post_text:
🌟 Excited to share my experience at the Adya Launch Lab at KIT - Kalaignarkarunanidhi Institute of Technology!

The session led by Shayak Mazumder, CEO & CTO of Adya.ai, along with the brilliant team, offered incredible insights into how platforms like Adya are empowering students and innovators to transform ideas into real-world AI solutions. Their demonstration highlighted how AI tools, guidance, and resources can support every stage of innovation—from ideation to execution.
I would also like to extend my gratitude to Dean Vimal Shanmuganathan, whose encouragement inspires students to explore emerging technologies and take on challenging projects. 🙏

💡 Building on this inspiration, I created an Agentic AI project:

“AutoML Agent: Autonomous Data Scientist” 🤖
This agent automates the entire data science workflow:

📥 Input:

User-uploaded dataset + query (e.g., “Analyze this dataset and suggest the best ML model”)

🛠 Workflow:

1. Dataset Analyzer Agent – Understands dataset structure, detects missing values, and identifies target variables

2. Data Cleaning Agent – Handles missing data, encodes categorical variables, and normalizes features

3. Model Selection Agent – Automatically selects suitable ML algorithms like Random Forest, Logistic Regression, or Gradient Boosting

4. Model Training & Evaluation Agent – Trains models and evaluates them with metrics like Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 Score, and Confusion Matrix

5. Insight Generator Agent – Provides actionable insights and recommendations

This project gave me hands-on experience with autonomous AI agents, demonstrating how AI can simplify complex data science workflows and generate meaningful insights efficiently. 🚀

Great Learning Experience at Adya.ai Launch Lab

Today I had the opportunity to attend the Adya.ai Launch Lab Programme, and it was an incredibly insightful session. The discussion helped me understand more about Artificial Intelligence, AI platforms, and how developers can build intelligent automation systems using modern AI tools.
During the session, I explored the Adya.ai platform, which allows developers to create AI-powered workflows and applications efficiently.

As part of my learning, I also built a small AI-powered News Assistant project.

In this project:
• I used an HTTP API request to fetch real-time news data.
• The data is processed and sent to Gemini AI, where the AI reads the structured JSON data.
• Gemini then analyzes and summarizes the news, converting raw API data into meaningful insights for users.

This project helped me understand how API integrations, AI models, and automation workflows can work together to create intelligent applications.
Sessions like this truly inspire me to explore more in the field of AI, automation, and real-world AI product development.

A special thanks to Shayak Mazumder – CEO & CTO, Adya.ai for sharing valuable insights and encouraging students to innovate with AI.
Looking forward to building more AI-driven solutions and learning continuously in this exciting field!

author: Arun Pandit ®
post_text:
Some people leave a strong impression the very first time you interact with them.
I first met Shayak Mazumder sir virtually a few years ago during a panel discussion at the E-Log Asia Summit, hosted by Max Henry and the Global Supply Chain Council (GSCC). 

At that time he was the CEO & Co-Founder of Eunimart, and what stood out immediately was his clarity of thought and structured approach to problem solving.

We later met in person while I was representing Hyphen SCS at the TiE Global Summit (TGS) hosted by TiE Hyderabad, and most recently again at the India AI Impact Summit 2026.

Very few people combine clarity, conviction and execution the way Shayak Mazumder sir does.

Today, as the CEO of Adya, he is building what I personally consider one of the most advanced yet practical AI platforms emerging from India.

Through platforms like ATMA and Vanij, Adya enables organizations to build and deploy AI-driven applications across domains such as retail, finance, insurance, education, and jobs — deployable across multiple clouds (AWS, Azure, GCP) and digital commerce networks like ONDC.

What impressed me most is the balance between sophistication and usability.
Adya’s ADI (Adya Deep Intelligence) models are often 20–40x more economical than traditional LLM deployments, while supporting powerful multi-agent workflows for cataloguing, search, inventory management, logistics, pricing, customer support and more.

Platforms like these can dramatically lower the barriers to AI adoption.
It is not just about large enterprises anymore.

Solutions like Adya can empower SMEs, startups, and even universities, making AI affordable, easy to deploy, and usable for the long tail of businesses and institutions.

During the summit, Shayak Mazumder sir also gave a live demonstration of the platform to students from my alma mater Sainik School Sujanpur Tira, while guiding them through the First Principles approach to problem solving — encouraging them to break problems down to their fundamentals and rebuild solutions from the ground up.

Personally, interacting with him always carries an added sense of respect. His Indian Navy background as a Lieutenant Commander and my Sainik School roots, create an instant bond built on discipline, service, and purpose.

Encouraging to see advanced yet easy-to-implement AI platforms coming out of India — with the power to democratize AI for the long tail.

#ShayakMazumder #AdyaAI #ArunPandit #HyphenSCS #MaxHenry #GSCC #AIImpactSummit #AgenticAI #ArtificialIntelligence #BuildInIndia #ONDC

2025 was the year I chose courage over comfort, the year I stopped shrinking myself to fit familiar spaces and started stepping into rooms that once intimidated me.

Pixels Creative Technologies launched its first project out of Australia Stemware Studio — a quiet reminder that dreams grow when you dare to move. We unveiled for our first AI client Shayak Mazumder Archana . at the NVIDIA Summit, and in that moment, the weight of every late night turned into pride. It wasn’t just a launch, it was proof that we belonged.

Meeting Sajith Ansar and witnessing his journey of building Unlimits showed me what conviction looks like in motion. At @GHCI, surrounded by relentless ambition and voices like Bhumi Pednekar, I saw the future of women led tech being bold, diverse, and unapologetic. Experienced transformative innovation and collaboration with world-class leaders at the IndiaAI Summit Sundar Pichai Jensen Huang Piyush Goyal Marc Benioff Dario Amodei.

The National Institute ofDesign (NID) Ahmedabad Alumni Meet brought me back to my roots. The round table at ATLAS SkillTech University and countless founder conversations reminded me that growth doesn’t happen in isolation - it happens when you show up, speak up, and lean in.

2025 wasn’t just about networking.
It was about rewriting the story I told myself about what was possible.

If you’re waiting for a sign to step forward, this is it.
The rooms you’re afraid to enter might just be the ones that change your life.

author: Rashi Gupta
post_text:
I've been listening to multiple sessions from the India AI Summit. And this line has been living in my head rent-free since:

"AI karlega kaam to, but kya karwana hai becomes important."
Shayak Mazumder said this. AI will do the work. But what work you get it to do — that's where the real thinking lives.

He shared stories from across domains — defence, healthcare, drug discovery, and education. His point: everybody should be able to build what they want to build. The masses are becoming creators. But understanding doesn't come until you actually go out and do the work. That's what makes first principles thinking non-negotiable.

Manik Pasricha shared similar thoughts. Companies like Emergent are generating $500K revenue per employee, 10x what traditional IT manages. You don't need a tech co-founder anymore. You can ship an MVP by thinking and prompting.

So if everyone can build, what's the edge?
Solving the customer problem backwards — starting with what customers actually need, not building forward an AI-native product hoping a problem shows up.

India is sitting on first-degree problems. Healthcare, governance, civic tech, consumer tech. High-impact opportunities that don't need AI washing. They need someone who understands the problem deeply enough to know where technology actually fits.

First principles thinking. Customer empathy. When everything else is replicable by AI, these become the differentiators.

Also, a big shoutout to IndiaAI for putting these sessions online and making these conversations accessible to anyone willing to listen.

#IndiaAISummit #AI #FirstPrinciples #BuildForIndia #ProductManagement

Honoured to participate in the India AI Impact Summit 2026 at Bharat Mandapam, New Delhi, where I spoke in the STPI-led session on “Social Empowerment via AI Accessibility.”

The discussion brought together diverse perspectives from technology, policy, and venture ecosystems, focusing on how AI can transcend efficiency to become a true equalizer for society. We explored practical pathways to enhance AI accessibility, inclusivity, and outcome-driven solutions for citizens, enterprises, and public systems.

From a FarEye perspective, this conversation holds particular significance. As we implement AI at scale across logistics and delivery ecosystems, we witness firsthand how intelligent platforms can enhance reach, reliability, and real-world access, rather than merely optimizing operations. Responsible, scalable AI can reinforce essential networks that people rely on daily.

It was a privilege to share the panel with distinguished leaders and exchange ideas on:
• Making AI usable and reachable at scale
• Responsible and inclusive AI deployment
• Bridging ecosystem gaps between innovation and impact
• Public–private collaboration for social good

I am grateful to the organizers for curating a meaningful and action-oriented dialogue, and to the audience for their thoughtful engagement and questions. The true success of AI will not only be measured by its capabilities but by who it empowers.

author: Varad Chalmale
post_text:
My first post about the India AI Impact Summit got me thinking.
If AI's future is being shaped in rooms like this, who's actually ready to be in those rooms?
I've been diving deep into this over the past few weeks.
And I'm noticing a pattern.
The conversation always starts with:
 "We want to deploy AI agents."
But within 5 minutes, it shifts to:
"Who's going to be accountable when it makes a mistake?"
"How do we know it's not hallucinating in our core workflows?"
"What happens when regulators ask us to explain a decision?"
Here's what I'm realizing:
The companies winning at AI aren't the ones with the best demos.
They're the ones who figured out governance before they scaled.
They're asking:
Who owns the agent's mistakes?
How do we audit what it's learning?
How do we ensure it doesn't become a compliance liability?
This is where the real work is.
Not in making AI smarter.
But in making AI trustworthy enough to run your business.
That's the gap between a prototype and production.
And honestly? It's the gap most companies are still ignoring.
At the India AI Impact Summit, I'm watching for one thing:
Who's talking about infrastructure, governance, and reliability.
Not just intelligence.
Because intelligence without accountability is just expensive guesswork.
If you're at the summit and thinking about these questions too, let's talk.
Hall 5, Pod 5 - MIB Pavilion | Feb 16-20 | 10 AM-6 PM
Adya Shayak Mazumder Amit Srivastava

The Mahakumbh of AI is happening. And it's in India.
On a random day over a "chai" conversation:
My cousin asked me, 'Who owns AI?'. 
I couldn't answer.
Not because I didn't know the technical answer.
But because the real answer is bigger.
AI isn't owned by one company. It's shaped by governments, researchers, policymakers, enterprises, and founders.
The people who shape AI, shape the future.
Here's why this moment matters.
For the first time, the minds influencing the global AI narrative are converging in India.
At the India AI Impact Summit 2026.
If the Mahakumbh brings together millions seeking direction once in a lifetime, this summit brings together the minds directing where billions of lives are headed.
This isn't just another conference.
It's a signal that India isn't just consuming AI, we're helping define its direction.
When global AI conversations move from closed boardrooms to Indian soil, that's geopolitical shift. That's economic power.
If you care about AI, pay attention.
Because it's not just about models anymore.
 It's about governance. Ownership. Power.
Rooms like this determine who leads the next decade.
My cousin asked, "Who owns AI?"
Maybe the better question is: Who gets a seat at the table where AI's future is shaped?

I built an AI agent — and it changed how I think about business decisions.
During Adya Launch Lab, I designed and built a Decision Debt Agent — an agent that identifies delayed or postponed business decisions and quantifies their hidden financial impact.
The idea was simple, but powerful:
What if every delayed decision came with a visible price tag?
🧠 What the Decision Debt Agent does:
Detects decision delays (hiring, expansion, investments, migrations)
Models the downstream impact on productivity and revenue
Converts delay into measurable decision debt (₹ per year)
Surfaces clear, actionable recommendations instead of vague warnings
⚙️ What I learned building this agent:
AI systems aren’t just about responses — they’re about accountability
Agents work best when they combine logic, structure, and context
Real-world AI needs to be explainable, auditable, and decision-ready
Using Adya’s agent-first platform, I moved beyond prompt-level experimentation and focused on:
Designing agent workflows
Structuring inputs and outputs
Thinking in terms of production constraints and system behavior
Grateful to Adya and Shayak Mazumder sir for creating a space where students learn to build AI systems, not just talk about them.
Onward to building agents that make better decisions visible 🚀

author: Preetha Murugesan
post_text:
On 27.01.26, I attended an ADYA session that really resonated with how I’ve always liked to approach AI.

I’ve consistently believed AI projects should go beyond technical success and actually help real people in meaningful ways. That session reinforced this mindset - AI isn’t just about what models can do, but about what people still want to understand.

Doctors do explain medical reports, and they do it well. But people are naturally curious. After an appointment, many still wonder:
What does this value really mean for my body?
What could happen in the future if this continues?
What small changes can I start making today?
That’s where I saw space for AI to support, not replace, medical professionals.

With this approach, I built ReportWise – an AI-powered Medical Report Explainer.

The goal wasn’t just to read reports. It was to make them easier to live with and learn from.
ReportWise:
– extracts lab test data from uploaded PDFs and report images
– identifies whether values are normal or need attention using calm visual indicators
– explains each test in simple, everyday language
– describes possible general effects if certain levels remain abnormal
– suggests practical lifestyle and wellness habits people can follow
– presents everything in a structured dashboard so users see insights, not just numbers

This project reminded me that AI is powerful not just because it can analyze data, but because it can patiently answer the extra questions people carry after the doctor’s visit.

The Adya session didn’t change what I believe about AI — it strengthened it. It pushed me to think more like a product builder and focus on clarity, usefulness, and trust.

Still refining, still learning — but always building toward AI that helps people understand more, not feel more confused.

Grateful to the Adya team, and Shayak Mazumder for encouraging builders to move beyond demos and focus on deployable, user-centric AI.

post_text:
I recently worked on a full-stack notes sharing platform for students in Adya, designed to solve a persistent academic problem: fragmented study materials, lack of organization, and inefficient peer-to-peer sharing.
🔹 Student Notes Sharing Platform (EdTech Use Case)
The platform enables students to upload, manage, and access study notes in a centralized system, organized by subject and contributor.
I designed and implemented the system as a fully functional web application with both frontend and backend components.
Key capabilities of the platform include:
Secure note uploads with metadata (title, subject, contributor)
Backend-powered storage and retrieval of notes
Subject-wise browsing and filtering for faster access
Download functionality for shared resources
A structured upload workflow with validation and persistence
From a system perspective, the project covers:
Frontend UI built for clarity and usability
Backend APIs to handle note management
Database integration for structured storage
Separation of concerns between presentation, logic, and data
The platform reduces dependency on scattered messaging apps and cloud links, providing a single source of truth for academic notes.
💡 This project reflects my interest in building end-to-end systems, not just interfaces—focusing on real workflows, data flow, and scalability considerations in educational technology.

author: Kamaleshwaran Bm
post_text:
I recently worked on two AI-driven problem statements that focus on solving high-impact challenges across banking and public healthcare.
🔹 AI Tractor Quotation Analyzer (Banking Use Case)
The first solution addresses a real operational challenge for an HDFC Bank–style lending workflow, where tractor loan quotations arrive in highly unstructured formats (images, PDFs, handwritten scans).
I designed a violet-themed AI interface that represents the problem statement, enabling automated extraction of critical fields such as:
Tractor Model
Horsepower (HP)
Quotation Amount
This system reduces manual verification, speeds up loan processing, and improves accuracy using AI-based document understanding.
🔹 AI Healthcare Prevention Agent using Satellite Data
The second solution is an AI agent (chatbot) focused on healthcare prevention, powered by satellite data insights.
The agent analyzes environmental and satellite-derived indicators to:
Identify potential health risk patterns
Support early prevention strategies
Deliver actionable insights through a conversational interface
This approach bridges remote sensing + AI + public health, aiming for proactive healthcare rather than reactive treatment.
💡 These projects reflect my interest in building AI systems that go beyond models — systems that solve real, scalable problems in finance and healthcare.
I thank Adya for providing a wonderful application that saves a lot time

This wasn’t just about building an AI project — it was about rethinking how GenAI can solve real operational problems in banking.
Working on a GenAI-powered Tractor Invoice Processing system made one thing very clear:
 👉 AI isn’t about extracting text — it’s about understanding messy real-world data and turning it into trusted decisions.
In this project, I moved beyond simple OCR and focused on end-to-end problem solving:
 • Designing an OCR + GenAI pipeline for unstructured, handwritten dealer quotations
 • Extracting critical fields like Tractor Model, Horsepower, and Quotation Amount from inconsistent formats
 • Adding validation logic to catch mismatches, missing data, and risky inputs
 • Building confidence scoring and human-in-the-loop checks for underwriting safety
What stood out wasn’t just the tech — it was the execution mindset:
 Build for real bank workflows, not clean datasets
 Handle errors, ambiguity, and edge cases
 Think like a product, not just a model
 Automate → validate → review → approve
This project reinforced a simple truth:
 If your AI can’t handle real-world messiness, it’s not production-ready.
Building this gave me a stronger understanding of how GenAI can support faster, safer loan approvals by reducing manual effort while maintaining trust and compliance.

An AI system creates real value only when its outputs are understandable, reliable, and usable by decision-makers.
Next steps involve continued refinement, validation, and deployment of ResumeIntel.

Designing multi-agent intelligence (memory observation + prediction & nudge agents)
Turning passive reminders into proactive, risk-based nudges

Build faster without getting stuck in infrastructure
Think in products, not just prototypes
Launch → validate → iterate → scale
Create solutions that are market-ready, not just assignment-ready

 If you’re not deploying and testing real use cases, you’re just consuming.

 less theory, more systems thinking,

Welcome to our episode -2 of "Meet the CEO"

Meet the CEO takes you beyond the press releases to discover how today’s business leaders really think, decide, and lead—with honest stories and practical insights you can use in your own career and life.” It reveals the human side of leadership—the doubts behind bold decisions, the failures that shaped success, and the real people navigating the pressure of leading organizations.
 
The idea is that by each episode you as an audience would be feeling both inspired and equipped with something concrete you can think about or try.

In this episode of Positive Momentum India, Ashish Mehra sits down with Shayak Mazumder for an inspiring conversation!

Highlights of the discussion

🟥 AI is humanity's next fire. Shayak wrestles with whether landing on the moon or building LLMs is the bigger discovery. AI will reshape the world as we know it—it's possibly the most significant breakthrough since fire itself.

🟥 Democratization is the mission. Just like the internet and cloud before it, AI risks becoming controlled by a few. India needs platforms that enable equitable building—not another feudal system that stifles global growth and innovation.

🟥 Enterprise AI is genuinely broken. Large enterprises want to adopt AI but face real barriers. ADYA is building India's largest and possibly the world's most advanced open AI ecosystem to solve this—with 20+ enterprise clients including HUL and Canara Bank already deployed.

🟥 Self-taught doesn't mean self-limited. Never went to college. Started in the Indian Navy, got an MBA from INSEAD, built 35 enterprise platforms across insurance, credit, recruitment, retail, and FMCG. Teaching AI while building it. Proof that unconventional paths create exceptional outcomes.

🟥 Dream big or regret it forever. Victor Hugo had it right: the biggest risk isn't dreaming big and failing—it's dreaming small and achieving it. By the time you reach that small dream, you'll realize it's the worst thing that could have happened.

It's a conversation about ambition at scale, technological equity, and building the future with conviction—not just participating in it.

To get the full conversation, check out our podcast episode:- https://lnkd.in/gpyCFcgF

here's what it taught me about B2B sales.

author: Christina S. Saad
post_text:
Should Healthcare Enterprises Build Their Own AI Agents?

I spent Tuesday evening at AlleyCorp hearing Shayak Mazumder (Adya) break down what's actually working with AI agents in enterprise. 

The reality check: Recent MIT research shows 95% of enterprise AI implementations struggle to deliver measurable results, despite billions in investment. The issue isn't the technology—it's that companies don't know where their data is going or how to use these tools effectively.

The insight that stuck with me:

For healthcare providers and large enterprises, building domain-specific agents might be more sustainable than buying off-the-shelf solutions. Here's why:

- They run on internal data and the enterprise's own servers (critical for HIPAA compliance and security).
- They're more cost-effective and energy-efficient because they're scoped to specific workflows.
- With tools like Adya, the barrier to entry is dropping fast.

Shayak's rule of thumb:

If you're doing something once, let the model handle it. But if it's a repetitive workflow? Take the time to build a tailored agent on a competent model. The answer isn't always a bigger, fancier model. It's about building a stronger, more focused agent.

His framework for building agents: MBA—Memory (data/context), Brain (how to think), and Action (what to do).

Why this matters for healthcare:

Provider groups scaling through M&A face a data integration nightmare. Patient records, billing systems, and scheduling platforms are all fragmented across acquisitions. The MSOs and health systems that figure out how to build lightweight, secure, domain-specific agents will have a massive operational advantage.

The question isn't whether AI will reshape healthcare operations. It's whether providers will build their own tools or get locked into expensive, generic solutions that don't fit their workflows. 

---

Thanks to Seth Hidek for organizing, and Fabio Valle and Robert Maciejko for their continued leadership of the INSEAD AI community!

author: MADHANKUMAR S
post_text:
Ever wished test automation could think, plan, and fix itself?
Now it can. Introducing my 🤖 20th Agent — Playwright AI Test Suite🌟, an End-to-End AI Testing Agent built entirely inside the Vanij Playground🚀🎉
✨ What It Does:
 Playwright AI Test Suite combines planning, script generation, and self-healing in one seamless workflow:
📂 Flow 1: Seed & Plan
 1️⃣ Upload test specifications (CSV/TXT/JSON)
 2️⃣ Planner agent parses files, injecting data via String Replacement into Gemini prompts
 3️⃣ Gemini generates structured Markdown Playwright test plans ready for automation
📂 Flow 2: Generator
 4️⃣ Converts Markdown plans into executable Playwright scripts (JS/TS) with selectors, assertions, and descriptive test titles
 5️⃣ Developers can refine scripts in real-time via chat input
📂 Flow 3: Healer
 6️⃣ Upload failing test logs or screenshots
 7️⃣ Healer agent parses failures, injects them into Gemini prompts, and auto-generates corrected scripts
 8️⃣ Output is instantly reviewable — no more manual debugging
🎯 Why It Matters:
 Traditional test automation is slow, repetitive, and error-prone. Playwright Copilot:
Dynamically generates test plans from any fixture/spec file
Creates production-ready scripts with minimal effort
Instantly debugs failing tests inside the Vanij Playground
Enables seamless end-to-end AI-driven testing
💡 Use Cases:
 ✔️ Rapid test plan creation for new features
 ✔️ Automated Playwright test script generation for web apps
 ✔️ Debugging failing tests in CI/CD pipelines
 ✔️ Reducing QA cycles and improving reliability
💥 Unique Edge:
 Unlike static VS Code setups or standalone CI/CD tests, Playwright Copilot chains planning, generation, and self-healing into a single AI-driven agent — bringing speed, intelligence, and efficiency to QA teams.
🙏 Huge thanks to sushma billu, Shayak Mazumder and the Adya AI Team for empowering me to push automation, AI reasoning, and testing efficiency to the next level.
This is my 🌟 20th milestone agent, advancing AI-driven Playwright testing for developers worldwide! 🌟

author: Basil Saji
post_text:
𝗧𝘂𝗿𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗽𝗮𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗼 𝗔𝗜 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸𝗳𝗹𝗼𝘄𝘀 ⚡🧠

AI is often discussed in terms of models and capabilities, but its real value emerges when it is embedded into operational workflows. That perspective became much clearer through the Adya Launch Lab experience.

During the Adya buildathon, our team — Albert M Paul, ALWIN SONY, Abhinavkrishna B, and myself — developed TractoLens, an AI-driven tractor invoice processing system designed for loan underwriting workflows. The objective was to handle a problem that appears straightforward but is operationally complex due to handwritten invoices, inconsistent formats, and manual verification steps.

Built using Adya Flow Builder and multimodal LLMs, TractoLens:
• extracts structured data from handwritten and printed invoices
• produces strict, schema-controlled JSON outputs (model, HP, price)
• validates entries against a master reference dataset
• flags inconsistencies for human review rather than silently passing errors

The system was designed with a workflow-first mindset — ingestion → intelligence → validation → action — focusing on reliability, auditability, and reduced manual effort in underwriting processes.

Appreciate the insights from Shayak Mazumder sir on designing AI systems that address real business constraints, and the Adya team for fostering a build environment cantered on practical problem-solving.

author: Khwahish Goel
post_text:
What if mothers didn’t have to search so hard for support?

At HackHer, we chose to build Willow because there are simply not enough accessible resources for mothers to get the care and support they need. Care is often fragmented, hard to find, and carried alone—and we wanted to help change that.
Working in a space created by women, for women was incredibly comforting and motivating. It pushed us to build a project that directly supports women in a meaningful, tangible way.

What Willow does:
It is a support hub where mothers can find help—and where others can step in to give it.
Locate nearby clinics and medical resources 
Find babysitters and childcare support
Volunteer to support mothers who need an extra hand

On technical side, we built a clean, calming frontend using React, HTML, and CSS, designed to make finding support feel simple and welcoming. On the backend, we used Vertex AI to locate nearby clinics and medical resources, integrating mapping tools so care is easy to discover when it’s needed most. 

Huge shout out to my amazing teammates—Adya Joshi and Khushi Kakar for making this project come to life, and to the HackHer sponsors—Major League Hacking, Manning College of Information and Computer Sciences, UMass Amherst for creating a space where ideas like Willow can grow.

author: Madhu Venugopal
post_text:
The Real Battleground in Healthcare

Every 1% reduction in SG&A for a national payer equals hundreds of millions in structural value.

That doesn’t come from AI pilots.
It doesn’t come from copilots.
It doesn’t come from incremental automation.

It comes from platform-level rewiring.

The kind that:

✔ Collapses silos instead of integrating them
✔ Standardizes workflows instead of customizing chaos
✔ Orchestrates the provider lifecycle end-to-end
✔ Eliminates exception-driven operations
✔ Creates enterprise-scale operating leverage

Generative AI and agentic AI do not fix fragmentation.

Without scale and operating model redesign, AI simply shifts cost:

Human labor ➝ Machine labor.

Payroll drops.
Technology spend rises.
The waste stays.

That is not transformation.
That is cost displacement.

No number of copilots will fix structural inefficiency.

This is precisely why Infosys Helix is more relevant today than ever.

Because sustainable advantage in healthcare will not come from deploying more AI.

It will come from eliminating administrative friction at the core.

Until structural simplification happens,
the $3T administrative illusion continues.

author: Harshit Singh
post_text:
I got a chance to attend something I’ll probably remember for a long time — and not just because of who was on stage, but because of everything that happened around it.

It started at Indian Institute of Technology Delhi. I walked in with a notebook I didn’t end up using once. Listening to Sam Altman in conversation with Vinod Khosla and Raghav Gupta felt like hearing thoughts I’d had in fragments suddenly come together clearly.

One idea stayed with me — AI isn’t another tech wave like mobile or cloud.
It’s more like electricity. A shift so fundamental that everything reorganizes around it.

When comfort was called out as the real risk, it hit personally. It’s easy to feel productive while staying safe in what you already know.

Right after that, the OpenAI session at the India AI Impact Summit expanded the picture — talking about skills, education, and how humans grow alongside intelligence that’s becoming programmable. Not fear. Preparation.

What made it even more real was connecting with the Gaurav Tripathi from Partex.AI Technology and researchers working with Google DeepMind .
Since we’re building in AI for drug discovery, our conversations about protein structures, antibody engineering, and accelerating biology felt like glimpsing the future happening quietly behind the scenes.

We also attended the AI in democracy session — listening to perspectives from across the world: Chinmay Pandya,
Om Birla,Martin Chungong ,Lajos Olah, and
Jimena Sofia all exploring how technology, trust, and governance intersect.

At one point, I saw N. Chandrababu Naidu walking through the stalls, speaking with innovators about real-world solutions — and it grounded everything. Big ideas only matter when they turn into action.

Behind all this was another story.

I was coordinating the entire visit for 40+ students as the point of contact. There were missed calls, timing issues, running between entry points, constant check-ins, and moments where I genuinely wondered if we’d pull it off smoothly.

It wasn’t easy.
But we did.

And watching everyone experience these conversations, ask questions, and leave inspired made every stressful moment worth it.

Huge thanks to Rajeev Kumar Singh , Ph.D and Shiv Nadar University for making this opportunity possible.

On the ride back home, tired but smiling, one thought stayed with me:

This isn’t just another tech trend.
We’re watching the foundations of a new era being built.

And the real choice is simple —
will we just use it… or will we build it?

author: Hari Easwaran Iyer
post_text:
While I thank Dr  Adya Sharma, Director SCMS, for her kind words I am reposting this to the environment to draw the attention of Industry Leaders , the  Academia and the decision making Authorities.
During our informal interactions, Dr Adya Sharma (who is doing phenomenal work in steering young minds into future leaders ) did mention the time is ripe when there is a ‘top down’ approach to steering education in India. Industry support in ensuring that students are ‘employment ready’ especially during current times where the AI challenge is greater than ever. Sharing some forward looking thoughts..
(A) AI is human created hence we need to make sure that there is always a AI savvy human who is driving AI. When ‘AI drives AI’ it will be a disaster waiting to unfold. Hence the need for a sweet spot between ‘ethics and (de)regulation’. We need to upscale to ensure that only ‘AI savvy humans’ replace non AI savvy humans ( and not AI replacing humans) in the work place.
(B) Need for Industry to map with the academic curriculum and if required fund it to make the curriculum Industry friendly so as to encompass changes which ensure  that the creative power of students is not lost to ready made algorithms. (LLM’s have already got ahead of human ‘memory’.) As such our rote based eduction system ( from primary education itself ) merits a serious relook. The pristine ‘essay’ type questions need to make a comeback and complement  “objective type ‘ questions/answers.
(C) Time is rife when we need embrace an eduction system which moves away from fostering  a  ‘Winner  takes  it All’  syndrome to ‘Something for All’… simply because individual excellence is giving way to ‘team excellence’
(D) A request to Industry leaders to come forward and talk to the super bright students of such illustrious universities  ( SCMS being among the foremost) and share their experiences because  ‘True Stories’ ( of both winning and losing) has  always been the best currency in which humanity trades. Finally, my compliments to Dr Adya Sharma,  for imparting meaningful education with emphasis on specific (character based) skillsets which motivate and provide equal opportunity to all students. Kudos again for these are times when students will not hold themselves hostage to a fixed routine unless they are meaningful and resonate with their inherent ability to excel.

author: Charu Jain
post_text:
Reflecting on the CII panel at the India AI Impact Summit 2026, moderated by Gautam (GRAIT Cloud), the recurring theme was that AI is becoming the new operating system for brands. It’s not just a “tool” to be bolted on; it’s a transformative force that reshapes how brands create, distribute, personalise, and measure everything from end to end.

A few key takeaways from the panel:

1) Creativity and AI are evolving from campaigns to a continuous system. Brands are no longer just “making ads”; they’re building always-on experiences where creativity can scale responsibly.

2) Offline and small models offer significant opportunities. Small language models (SLMs) that function without internet access can dramatically alter reach stories.

3) AI can finally connect with populations that traditional media couldn’t. With the right language, cost, and distribution strategies, rural and underserved audiences become accessible in ways that were previously impractical.

4) India-specific research can become a real advantage. One particularly intriguing discussion revolved around using AI to accelerate the understanding of Indian genetics, enabling therapies and drugs to be more effectively targeted for our demographic, rather than being copied from global averages.

5) Cost unpredictability is a major barrier to adoption. While the “pay-per-token” model is suitable for experimentation, it often feels like an open-ended meter for MSMEs. Small businesses don’t just need low costs; they need predictable costs. This predictability is what transforms curiosity into actual adoption.

Niraj Ruparel (Wpp), Shayak Mazumder (Adya), Manish (Oracle), and Rakesh (JetSynthesys) provided grounded perspectives, ranging from enterprise readiness to real-world scale and user expectations.

Overall, the takeaway is that if we want AI to truly spread across India, we should focus less on “access” and more on outcomes that are reliable, affordable, and predictable.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on the biggest barrier to AI adoption for MSMEs right now: skills, trust, or cost predictability?

Secured 3rd Overall & 1st in B.Tech Category at DevJam – Weekend of Code, MNNIT
We are drowning in fragmented information.
One portal for grades.
WhatsApp for notices.
Word-of-mouth for everything else.

We looked at this chaos and realized: students don’t need another portal — they need a system to save them.
That’s why we built Campus Chanakya — a digital brain to navigate your life within the campus.

What is Campus Chanakya?
An AI-powered campus management platform that acts as a strategic advisor for students, professors, and administration — bringing clarity to campus life through intelligent automation.

How we built it
We broke the campus ecosystem into three tightly connected pillars — students, professors, and admins — with Chanakya as the AI layer guiding each one.

Our focus was on:
Turning scattered data into actionable insights
Using AI to summarize, prioritize, and advise, not just respond
Designing workflows that reflect real campus life
Keeping everything secure, scalable, and simple to use

My contribution
I worked primarily on the backend and API side, handling system architecture, AI integrations, and authentication. I also helped coordinate feature flow and integrations across the team, while serving as the team lead during the build.

Tech Stack
Python | FastAPI | Gemini API | SQLite | JWT | Argon2 | HTML | CSS | JavaScript

post_text:
■Sarvam AI surpasses Google Gemini & ChatGPT in India specific requirements: The news matters for India :
●In certain specialized areas, Sarvam AI has shown superior performance compared to Google Gemini and ChatGPT. These areas are primarily focused on the Indian ecosystem. 
Sarvam AI surpasses these competitors in the following fields:
●Indic Language Understanding & Generation: Sarvam AI models, including Sarvam-1 and Sarvam-M, are designed for more than 10 Indian languages. They perform better than larger models in these languages. These models excel at understanding code-mixed, conversational, and regional language nuances that global models often miss.
●Real-World Speech Recognition (Voice-First AI): Sarvam Audio has shown higher accuracy than Gemini 3 Flash and GPT-4o in transcribing Indian speech, particularly with “code-mixed” and conversational, multi-speaker audio.
●Document Intelligence in Context: According to early 2026 reports, Sarvam Vision surpasses Gemini 3 Pro and GPT 5.2 in document intelligence, specifically in understanding and processing diverse, complex, and multilingual documents.
●Token Efficiency for Indic Scripts: Existing models often struggle with Indian languages, requiring 4-8 tokens per word. Sarvam's models are optimized to have a much lower fertility rate (1.4-2.1 tokens per word), making them more efficient and cost-effective.
●Specialized Technical/Domain Tasks: In benchmarks related to actuarial science, accounting, and higher-level mathematics (e.g., linear algebra), Sarvam AI has shown to outperform GPT-5 and Gemini. 
Key Takeaway: While Gemini and ChatGPT are generally better at English-related, universal tasks, Sarvam AI’s advantage lies in being a "Bharat-first" model that delivers higher accuracy in Indian-specific contexts and multilingual, voice-based applications.

author: Ramesh Narayan
post_text:
APAC Confluence was a timely reminder: AI success is less about pilots, and more about operating models, trust, and adoption.

5 key takeaways from APAC Confluence:
1) AI is shifting from pilots to real operating models
 The repeated message was clear: the experimentation phase is largely behind us. The advantage now comes from turning AI into a repeatable capability with standard patterns, scalable platforms, and business outcomes that can be measured.

2) Trust is the unlock for scale, not a box to tick
 Across sessions, trust showed up as the accelerator. Governance, security-by-design, and transparency are not “compliance overhead.” They are what make adoption possible, and adoption is what drives value.

3) Customer experience is being redesigned for an LLM-first world
 Many leaders highlighted that the customer journey no longer begins on a website. People are starting with LLMs, which changes how enterprises think about content, discovery, personalization, and digital engagement end-to-end.

4) Culture and skills will determine who moves fastest
 The biggest constraint is rarely the model. It’s adoption: mindsets, change fatigue, unclear guardrails, and workflows that weren’t built for AI. The most practical moves were heavy investment in training, role-based enablement, and pushing accountability into the business rather than keeping AI “centralized in IT.”

5) The winners will be the ones who combine speed with discipline
 A consistent theme was ambitious execution without losing control. Move fast where you can, set non-negotiables where you must, and keep learning loops tight. That balance is becoming a true differentiator.

For more insights https://l1nq.com/3GZhd

A winning start and a strong finish to day 2 at #APACConfluence2026

Day 2 kicked off with an insightful breakfast roundtable in Melbourne, featuring leaders who are moving beyond conversation to actively shape AI’s impact on growth, talent, and customer experience.

This was followed by an inspiring opening session from cricket great Glenn McGrath, who reflected on excellence, resilience, and leadership shaped by decades at the highest level of sport.

The rest of the day unfolded through a series of impactful sessions, some of which were:

- A deep dive into reimagining experiences with AI that explored AI‑powered personalization, automation, and immersive technologies. Moderated by Wendy Mak, (MUFG Pension & Market Services), the discussion featured Sumit Virmani (Infosys), Duncan Egan (Adobe), and Nathan Gumley (Telstra).

- Inderpreet Sawhney (Infosys) moderated a session on balancing speed, scale, and responsibility of AI capturing perspectives from Jason Paris (One New Zealand), Sanjeev Gupta (HBF Health), and Sabina Janstrom (Lactalis Australia).

- A thought‑provoking session with Andy Nunn, a mentalist and mind shifter, exploring how perception, bias, and mindset influence performance and decision‑making.

- Andrew Melouney, Woodside Energy, shared Woodside’s journey toward becoming an Enterprise AI organization, moving beyond experimentation to real business impact.

- Security as the foundation of trust and transformation, a session moderated by Anant R Adya (Infosys), with insights from Aaron Bailey MCyber, MAICD of The Missing Link, Onur Taylor of Ventia, and Geeta Thakorlal, BHP, on scaling innovation with confidence and compliance.

The event concluded with a thoughtful valedictory address by Raja Shah (Infosys), bringing two days of insights and conversations to a close.

Thank you to everyone for making this a successful Confluence! infy.com/YjNkt

The system is designed to support CFOs and CIOs with data-driven financial decision-making, leveraging machine learning, predictive analytics, and explainable AI enabled through the Adya AI platform.
🔹 System Focus
 An AI-powered platform for IT spend analysis, operational budget planning, and resource allocation, delivering predictive insights and variance intelligence across departments, projects, and resource types.
🔹 Key Capabilities
 ✔️ Historical IT spend analysis to identify trends and patterns
 ✔️ ML-based quarterly budget forecasting with confidence intervals and risk assessment
 ✔️ Scenario-based forecasting for multiple planning assumptions
 ✔️ Automated variance reporting (actual vs forecast) with root-cause analysis
 ✔️ Executive dashboards tailored for CFO and CIO decision-making
 ✔️ Report exports in PDF, Excel, and interactive formats
🔹 Intelligent Forecasting Engine
 ✔️ Time-series and trend-based ML models trained on historical spend
 ✔️ Continuous learning through feedback loops
 ✔️ Explainable AI to ensure transparency in predictions
🔹 Enterprise Integration
 ✔️ Secure API integrations with ERP, accounting, and procurement systems
 ✔️ Real-time data synchronization and validation
 ✔️ Support for standard financial data formats
🔹 User Experience & Design
 ✔️ Dashboard-style UI with card-based KPIs
 ✔️ Clean, professional color scheme for clarity and trust
 ✔️ Interactive charts, drill-down analytics, and natural language explanations
 ✔️ Responsive layout for seamless access across devices
This exercise strengthened my understanding of how AI systems are architected for enterprise finance, balancing accuracy, transparency, scalability, and usability.

author: Avnee Saraogi
post_text:
Three years ago, on my Induction day at Symbiosis Centre for Management Studies- Pune, my father looked at the newly elected Student Council on stage and told me, “I want to see you up there one day.”

This week, during our Farewell Ceremony, that wish came full circle. 🎓

Standing on that stage to be felicitated for my tenure in the Student Council was emotional, but being conferred with the 'Best Sportsperson (Female)' of the Batch of 2026 was the cherry on top. It was a moment of pride not just for me and my family, but for the grit and discipline I’ve maintained over the last three years, managing academics and competitive sports.

SCMS Pune has been more than just an institute; it’s been a growth engine.

🌟 The Faculty: For pushing me in ways I was unaware of and constantly encouraging me to challenge my perspectives
🌟 The Environment: Being part of a student-driven culture where initiative is the only currency.
🌟 The Grind: Serving as the Sports Representative was my biggest classroom. From managing diverse teams, collaboration amidst friction, navigating complex documentation to the adrenaline of organizing Invictus, the largest intra-college event, it honed my management skills in ways a textbook never could.

None of this would have been possible without the incredible people by my side. To my friends who were my sounding boards through every late-night planning session, and my well-wishers who believed in my vision, thank you. You turned the challenges into memories and the hard work into a shared victory. Stuti Sampriya Aryan Patel Aanjaney Kapoor Prakhar Goel Amogh Kolsur

A special shout-out to my Basketball team; the court is where I truly learned about camaraderie, discipline, and fighting for every point until the final whistle. You all made the wins sweeter and the losses easier to bear.

As I step ahead into the professional world, I carry with me the lessons of resilience, leadership, and the ability to balance high-stakes responsibilities with academic excellence.

Thank you, Symbiosis Centre for Management Studies- Pune Dr Adya Sharma, for shaping the person I am today. Here's to new beginnings and continued growth! 🚀

author: Pratik Sonawane
post_text:
A Decade of Speed, Strategy, and a BW 40 Under 40 Win!
Reflecting on the last decade, I’m filled with an overwhelming sense of gratitude. Receiving the BW Marketing World 40 Under 40 "Future Masters" 2025 Award in New Delhi a fortnight ago was not just a career milestone; it was a powerful validation of a dual life lived at full throttle.

For me, the boardroom and the racetrack have never been separate worlds. They are two sides of the same coin, both demanding relentless precision, split-second decision-making, and the courage to push past the "impossible."

Since 2012, when I entered the world of marketing and entrepreneurship, I’ve been chasing two parallel dreams - Marketing & Motorsport.
In Marketing: From my formative years at Symbiosis Centre for Management Studies- Pune with Dr. Ashutosh Mathur and Dr Adya Sharma, to winning the Marketing Strategy and Leadership residential weekend at the Westminster Business School University of Westminster, I’ve been obsessed with building ecosystems that scale.

In Motorsport: That same drive fueled my journey on the track. Balancing high-stakes campaigns with high-speed corners wasn't easy, but it led to the ultimate reward: becoming a National Motorsport Champion in 2023.
Winning on both fronts, earning three national motorsport awards and now being recognized as one of India's top 40 marketing leaders under 40 is a feeling of pure, ecstatic momentum. MRF The Federation of Motor Sports Clubs of India (FMSCI) Rayomand Polo Cup India

My career has been defined by incredible leadership and shared visions. From the early days with JLR, Tata Motors, and Volkswagen Group Motorsport India, to the transformative years scaling Global Institute of Sports Business India with India On Track, Gaurav Modwel, Neel Shah, Reuben Borah, and Pinky Gandhi, and working closely with The Premier League, every lap has taught me something new.

Now, alongside Ganesh Pande and Kiran Shetty, I am heading the marketing for Collective Heads Exp Mktg Sol Pvt Ltd, Brand Banana & TechXP India. We are radically upgrading digital marketing ecosystems, bringing a "performance mindset" to brand narratives.

A huge thank you to BW Marketing World for this honor. To my mentors Dr Donna, Dr Jaafar, Prof Mudra, Dr Kanyanee, Prof Felicity,  and the Westminster faculty to my colleagues, this award is a testament to the guidance I’ve received.

As this chapter unfolds, my eyes remain fixed on the horizon. Whether it's a marketing brief or a qualifying lap, I believe in one thing: "I would rather go too far, than not far enough."

Here’s to more adventure, more speed, and many more wins. 🏁🚀

On International Women’s Day, AACSB celebrates the women shaping the future of business education. Through the Women Advancing Management Education (WAME) affinity group, AACSB connects a global network of women leaders across business schools, creating space for mentorship, collaboration, and shared leadership.

This community will come together at the WAME meeting during ICAM 2026 in Seattle, where the 2026 Patricia M. Flynn Distinguished Woman in Business Education Award will be presented. Established in 2016, the award recognizes female thought leaders whose leadership, research, and educational initiatives have made a meaningful difference for women in business education.

Different countries. Different systems. Different realities.
But the mission stays the same.

From climate-resilient financial services reaching thousands of smallholder farmers, in Malawi — most of them women carrying the weight of farms, families, and futures, to helping design a digital transformation strategy and opening smarter financial pathways for women entrepreneurs, in Jordan, to building resilience and access to markets for women led MSMEs in Côte d'Ivoire.

We believe in standing beside the women who quietly hold economies together.

This year’s International Women’s Day theme — “Give to Gain” — feels especially close to home. ✨ 

Because across villages, cities, and entire countries, progress often begins with women supporting women. 

A shared opportunity. 
A hand extended. 
A system that finally opens a door.

This Women’s Day, we celebrate the resilience, determination, and quiet courage of women everywhere — from the women at Amarante, to those across our clients and partners, and the men who champion equity alongside them — all working tirelessly across the globe, balancing work, community, and household expectations.

Because sometimes, all a woman needs is one door opened, one chance taken, one push forward.

And from there- she does the rest. 💯

author: Mohana priya ravi
post_text:
This Women’s Day feels different for me.

It made me pause and reflect on something very personal.

Somewhere in the middle of growing up, building things, chasing goals, and navigating life in 20s… I realised how easy it is to drift away from your own center. Not intentionally. Just slowly, quietly. while trying to keep up with everything life throws at you.

Life becomes fast. Responsibilities grow. You’re juggling work, dreams, expectations, healing, and survival all at once.

And in that rush, sometimes you forget to stay rooted in who you truly are.

As a spiritual person, I’ve always known that whenever I feel misaligned, the way back is inward. 

Once my Guru said:
“Adya is within you, and not external to you.”
And that's something that stayed deeply with me...

Those words reminded me of something profound.
The divine feminine isn’t fragile. She is powerful, aware, resilient but most importantly, she is aligned. 

Adya, the primal feminine energy, the Shakti, is not something we search for outside. She already exists within every woman.
But the real journey is remembering her… especially when life gets loud.
So this Women’s Day, I’m not celebrating perfection or strength in the usual sense.
I’m celebrating the quiet journey of a woman learning to stay rooted in her feminine energy while navigating the chaos of life.

Returning to herself.
Realigning with her Shakti.
And remembering that the divine feminine she seeks… has always been within her.

reminding every woman out there to channel the goddess in them! 

celebrate you!

I was truly grateful for the invitation to speak at the Rutgers inaugural Wealth Management, Advising, and Planning (MAP) Symposium. It was a wonderful event that brought together professionals and alumni from across the industry to engage with Rutgers students and share perspectives on the many paths in wealth management.

One of the highlights for me was participating in the “Athlete to Advisor” panel. Athletics shapes you in ways that extend far beyond the field, teaching you lessons in discipline, resilience and teamwork, and I appreciated the opportunity to share my journey transitioning from Division I athletics to my role at Fox, Penberthy & Dehn. It was a privilege to speak alongside fellow panelists, Patrick Natale, Aaron Nelson, and Dr. Vaughn A. Calhoun, Ed.D, whose thoughtful words I was hanging onto with the rest of the room. (Win the day!)

Opportunities like this are also especially meaningful to me because they allow me to live out the mission of the Climb Pause Lift Foundation and give back to the next generation. To the students who introduced themselves afterwards - thank you. Your curiosity and enthusiasm was contagious! 

A special thank you to Dr. David Pedersen for the care and effort he put into organizing such a meaningful event, and to Dean Monica Adya, whose vision and passion for building this program was evident in every detail. The future is bright for wealth management!

author: Ranjana Maitra
post_text:
The U.S. Consulate General Mumbai in association with the Confederation of Indian Industry (CII) organized an exclusive seminar on “Doing Business in Delaware”, hosted in honor of a high-level delegation from the State of #Delaware, USA, led by Office of Delaware Governor Matt Meyer, the First Lady, and Secretary of State Charuni Patibanda-Sanchez. It was a privilege to be part of this important dialogue on 4th March 2026 at the U.S. Consulate General Mumbai . 

Delaware is home to more than 60% of Fortune 500 companies and is globally recognized as the leading jurisdiction for business incorporation. Its stable and predictable legal framework, anchored by the renowned Delaware Court of Chancery, along with a business-friendly regulatory environment and strong access to global capital markets, makes it a powerful gateway for international investors.

Key takeaways from the seminar:
• Clear positioning of Delaware as a strategic hub for global business and innovation, underpinned by strong U.S.–India bilateral ties and a vibrant Indian diaspora presence in Delaware
 • Deep insights into its corporate and legal framework for incorporation
 • Strong focus on investment and partnership opportunities across clean energy, life sciences, and advanced manufacturing
 • Emphasis on academic and innovation partnerships through the University of Delaware
 • Engagement with representatives from the Office of the Governor, Division of Corporations, leading law firms, industry bodies such as Delaware BioScience Association and NIIMBL, and senior education leaders

The networking session following the seminar was equally valuable. What stood out personally was the keen interest shown by the Secretary of State in learning more about the initiatives of the CII Indian Women Network (IWN). I also had meaningful conversations with colleagues from the University of Delaware regarding the Indian market and education initiatives. It was also wonderful to reconnect with members of the U.S. Consulate team.

Platforms like these reaffirm how institutional partnerships, when anchored by government leadership and industry collaboration, can meaningfully shape cross-border engagement. A productive morning of policy, partnerships, and possibilities.

Today, TWG Global announced the launch of RightsHelper, a ticketing analytics and operations platform, designed to address growing fragmentation and inefficiencies for teams, leagues, and commercial rightsholders in the global sports and live entertainment ecosystem.

Originally built for the Los Angeles Dodgers organization and now serving more than 40% of Major League Baseball (MLB) teams, RightsHelper replaces disconnected vendor stacks with a unified operating model, helping rightsholders manage pricing, inventory, forecasting, and distribution with greater discipline and clarity.

Looking ahead, RightsHelper will integrate capabilities from TWG AI, further strengthening predictive modeling and real-time decision support to turn ticketing strategy into a smarter, more coordinated engine for growth.

Before anyone sees a label or a finished product, organic farming tests you.

It tests your dedication, your perseverance, and most of all your integrity.
At Adya Organics, we’ve learned that staying truly organic often means choosing the harder path. 
We wait when the market wants speed, rejecting produce when consistency is questioned. We stand by our farmers even when climate, yield, or timelines don’t cooperate.

There are days when doing the right thing feels far more difficult than doing the easy thing.

But every time, we come back to the same place: the soil and the trust it demands.

Today I am here to listen and learn from my fellow organic farmers.
If you grow organically, at any scale —
what is one principle you’ve refused to compromise on, even when it cost you something?

#OrganicFarmers #OrganicFarming #Farming #sustainableFarming #Community #AdyaOrganics

author: Vaibhav Sharma
post_text:
Building ADYA — An Advanced AI Assistant for Real-World Use
Excited to share the first look at a project I’ve been developing: ADYA, an advanced desktop AI assistant designed to bring intelligent interaction, seamless automation, and a refined user experience into one unified system.
ADYA is still in its early development phase, but the foundations are strong. The goal is simple:
 to create an AI assistant that feels natural to interact with, understands context, and integrates deeply with everyday tools.
What started as a small experiment has grown through months of trial, error, learning, and iteration — countless failed builds, UI redesigns, and rewrites that ultimately shaped a stronger, cleaner, more reliable system.
🔹 Key Capabilities (Current Phase)
Conversational AI powered by an LLM-router architecture with support for Google Gemini and local models.
Voice Recognition & Real-time TTS for hands-free interaction.
Integrated Web Search for contextual, fast information retrieval.
Live Camera Feed Module for future computer-vision use cases.
Spotify Integration for direct music control from the interface.
System Monitoring (CPU, memory, network) updated in real time.
🔹 UI & Experience
Built using PyQt5, ADYA features a crisp multi-panel interface including:
Chat module
Voice visualizer
Camera view
Spotify widget
Dark-mode support
Custom modular widgets for modern interaction
🔹 Modular Architecture
Some of the core modules powering ADYA:
VoiceRecognitionThread
CameraThread
AdvancedTextToSpeech
LLMRouter
SpotifyService
SpacegradeEventHandlers
SystemMonitor
UserAuth for login & preferences
🔹 Tech Stack
Python • PyQt5 • Google Gemini API • psutil • numpy • matplotlib • qdarkstyle • qtawesome • requests • asyncio • NewsAPI
ADYA is evolving quickly, and I’m excited to continue improving it — from UI refinements to deeper integrations and more advanced AI capabilities. I’ll be refining and expanding ADYA with new modifications and features regularly, ensuring it becomes more capable with every update.

post_text:
Had the pleasure of giving a lecture on AI in Business at Ramaiah Institute Of Technology, representing Adya!
Never thought I’d be on stage without an instrument, but here we are. Over the past few months, I’ve somehow gotten used to being in front of a mic... though I still can’t tell if that’s a good thing or not 😂

Public speaking has always been right up there on my list of fears, somewhere between “tech glitches before a demo” and “forgetting the notes mid-song.”
But each time I’ve said yes to it, the fear has gotten a tiny bit smaller. Turns out, confidence isn’t something you suddenly have but it’s something you accidentally build while trying not to panic.

Still a long way to go, but every talk feels like progress.
If public speaking freaks you out too, then start small, mess up gloriously, laugh it off, and keep at it. You might just surprise yourself.

author: Ajaya Kumar Dash
post_text:
🌱 Rethinking Consultancy Support for Emerging NGOs

Support to Adya Education and Charitable Trust

Access to central schemes and structured funding opportunities is undoubtedly important for #NGO growth and long-term sustainability.

However, many newly established NGOs operate with limited financial resources but strong grassroots dedication and social commitment. For such organizations, upfront consultancy fees can often become a barrier rather than support.

If advisory services are structured on a success-based or post-funding model, it creates a more inclusive and practical pathway for small and emerging NGOs. This approach encourages:

✔ Shared responsibility
✔ Mutual trust
✔ Ethical collaboration
✔ Outcome-driven partnerships

Sustainable development is not only about accessing funds — it is about building transparent systems, measurable impact, and long-term institutional strength.

The future of the social sector depends on partnerships that grow together.

#NGO #CSR #SocialImpact #SustainableDevelopment #NonProfitLeadership #EthicalConsulting

author: Krishnaprasad C.
post_text:
What do women carry?

At our recent AadyaMaya gathering – “Summa Oru 4 Vaarthai”, we began with a simple exercise.

Everyone in the room was given a small card and asked to write down four words that capture what women carry.

At first, it felt like a light activity.

But as the cards began to fill up, the room slowly grew quieter.

Words started appearing across the cards:

Family.
Pregnancy.
Pain.
Unspoken logistics.
Responsibility.
Expectations.

And many more.

But what stayed with us wasn’t just the words.

It was the stories behind them.

It took courage for many women in the room to open up about the invisible weight they carry every single day — the things that rarely get spoken about, yet shape so much of their lives.

And for a few of the men who participated, it was a moment of reflection.

Because sometimes we assume we understand what women carry.

But the reality, when you truly listen, is far deeper.

The room held something powerful that evening, honesty, vulnerability, and the quiet willingness to listen to each other without judgement.

Conversations like these don’t just create awareness.
They create empathy.

A heartfelt thank you to everyone who showed up and trusted the space enough to share their words and stories.

And a special thank you to Spendflo for opening their doors and allowing us to host this gathering. Spaces matter, and the right space makes conversations like these possible.

Moments like these remind us why AadyaMaya exists, to create safe spaces where voices are heard, stories are shared, and perspectives expand.

Something tells me this conversation is far from over.

It deserves another edition.
More voices.
More stories.
More listening.

Because the more we listen, the more we understand.

And perhaps, the more compassionate the world becomes.

✨ If you had to describe “what women carry” in four words, what would they be?

𝗗𝗼 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗸𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝟭𝟬𝟬% 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗹𝗱𝗿𝗲𝗻 𝗱𝗿𝗮𝘄 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝗮𝘀𝗸𝗲𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝘀𝗸𝗲𝘁𝗰𝗵 𝗮 𝗳𝗮𝗿𝗺𝗲𝗿?
A turbaned man, a plough, a field under the sun. 𝘕𝘰𝘵 𝘰𝘯𝘦 𝘤𝘩𝘪𝘭𝘥 𝘪𝘯𝘴𝘵𝘪𝘯𝘤𝘵𝘪𝘷𝘦𝘭𝘺 𝘥𝘳𝘢𝘸𝘴 𝘢 𝘸𝘰𝘮𝘢𝘯.

And that fact is disturbing.
Because if our children’s imagination reflects our collective consciousness, then somewhere along the way, we have quietly erased the women who hold Indian agriculture together.

Women farmers in India are everywhere, bent over fields, sowing, transplanting, harvesting, saving seeds, tending livestock. They make up over 73% of rural women workers and nearly a third of the agricultural labour force. They perform close to 80% of farm work and lead over 90% of India’s shift toward natural farming thereby reducing costs, preserving soil health, and sustaining food security for a nation of more than 1.3 billion people.
Yet, in records and recognition, they are still called “helpers” or “labourers,” rarely cultivators. They lack financial autonomy and rarely enjoy any land rights.

At Adya Organics, this reality is personal. Because women are not just participants in our ecosystem, they are integral to it.
We work directly with women-led groups across regions near our Vedic-inspired farms in Dehradun, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, and Jharkhand. From sourcing A2 ghee and raw honey to growing organic fruits and vegetables, our focus has been on creating stable incomes, skill development, and long-term empowerment for rural women.

We know we cannot change the system overnight. But we believe recognition begins with intention, and empowerment begins with opportunity.
